pages:
Statement Taking — Offence Playbooks (5-Part)

AN GARDA SÍOCHÁNA — Mountjoy

STATEMENT TAKING — OFFENCE PLAYBOOKS
One 5-part playbook per offence, for every witness — follow the five parts, don’t deviate
Working reference — not legal advice — verify current wording before relying.

HOW TO USE. In the moment, pick the offence and work the five parts top to bottom. The points to prove
are woven in so you don’t have to hold them separately. The ‘by witness type’ line tells you what shifts for a
victim, an independent witness, or a Garda. Elements are indicative — verify the current section before
relying.

1

Statement Taking — Offence Playbooks (5-Part)

Assault — s.2 Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997
POINTS TO PROVE. Intentional or reckless application of force / causing apprehension of immediate force
· without consent · without lawful excuse.
Part

Focus & prompts (get them thinking)

1 Intro

Who the witness is; relationship to the parties; if suspect known, how/name/how
long.

2 Scene

Where, when (their words), lighting, who was present; lead-up — words, gestures,
who approached whom, alcohol/drugs. Begin ADVOKATE.

3 Incident

The act, blow by blow: what force / contact, or the threat that made them fear
immediate force; how (fist/push/etc.); their position; that it was unwanted (no
consent) and they didn’t start it (no lawful excuse). Exact words.

4 Impact

Any pain/mark (even if slight); fear or apprehension — the s.2(b) apprehension is
itself the offence even without contact.

5 Close-out

CCTV, BWC, clothing, phone footage; willingness; adopt.

By witness type. Victim: impact/harm, no consent, history, willingness. · Independent witness:
vantage/ADVOKATE, who started it, exact words, no stake. · Garda: role, grounds, what you personally
saw, BWC, arrest, scene.
THINK: Who was the aggressor? Was it force, or the fear of immediate force? Did they consent to any
contact — and had any ‘fight’ been agreed?

2

Statement Taking — Offence Playbooks (5-Part)

Assault causing harm — s.3 Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997
POINTS TO PROVE. An assault (as s.2) · causing harm — harm to body OR mind, including pain and
unconsciousness.
Part

Focus & prompts (get them thinking)

1 Intro

As s.2; note the witness’s ability to speak to injuries (victim) or what they saw
(eyewitness).

2 Scene

As s.2 — conditions, who present, lead-up, ADVOKATE.

3 Incident

Blow by blow: mechanism of each blow, body part struck, force, sequence, victim’s
position, restraint, defensive acts; weapon/object (describe, source, use); any
pressure to the neck / difficulty breathing (consider s.3A strangulation).

4 Impact

Every injury (location/type/size); loss of consciousness; developing bruising (followup photos); medical attendance/treatment; and the harm to the mind — fear, shock,
sleep, avoidance. ‘Harm’ includes both.

5 Close-out

Injury photos (+ 24–48h follow-up); medical statement/records consent; CCTV,
BWC, clothing, weapon; willingness; adopt.

By witness type. Victim: impact/harm, no consent, history, willingness. · Independent witness:
vantage/ADVOKATE, who started it, exact words, no stake. · Garda: role, grounds, what you personally
saw, BWC, arrest, scene.
NOTE. s.3 max penalty was increased and s.3A non-fatal strangulation added by the CJ(Misc Provisions)
Act 2023 — verify.
THINK: Don’t stop at the visible bruise — capture pain, any blackout, and the psychological harm; each
can satisfy ‘harm’. Hands to the throat? Think s.3A.

3

Statement Taking — Offence Playbooks (5-Part)

Public order — s.6 Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994
POINTS TO PROVE. Threatening, abusive or insulting words or behaviour · in a public place · with intent
to provoke a breach of the peace or reckless as to whether one may occur · plus the impact.
Part

Focus & prompts (get them thinking)

1 Intro

Who the witness is; where they were; their role (member on duty / member of the
public).

2 Scene

The public place — describe it and why it is public; time; who was around; the
atmosphere.

3 Incident

The exact words and behaviour — verbatim; who they were directed at; repetition;
the intent/recklessness (aimed, persisted after warning).

4 Impact

The effect — who was intimidated, alarmed, provoked, or feared a breach of the
peace, and how they felt (this is routinely missed and is essential).

5 Close-out

BWC (produce it!), CCTV; any warning given; willingness; adopt.

By witness type. Victim/public: how the words/behaviour made them feel (fear, intimidation, provocation).
· Independent witness: what was said/done and the reaction of others. · Garda: the public place, the words
verbatim, any warning, the demeanour, BWC.
THINK: Capture the IMPACT, not just the words — a s.6 statement with no effect on anyone is incomplete.
Verbatim words, and who heard them.

4

Statement Taking — Offence Playbooks (5-Part)

Criminal damage — s.2 Criminal Damage Act 1991
POINTS TO PROVE. Damaged property belonging to another · without lawful excuse · intending the
damage or reckless as to it.
Part

Focus & prompts (get them thinking)

1 Intro

Who the witness is; their interest in / ownership of the property; how they know it
was damaged.

2 Scene

Where the property is; its condition before; how they know it was undamaged before.

3 Incident

What was done and how (if witnessed) — the act, the method, who did it; or the
discovery and state found. No consent/lawful excuse to damage it.

4 Impact

The damage in detail (what, extent, cost/repair); any consequential effect (loss of
use, distress). Photograph it.

5 Close-out

Photos of the damage; CCTV, BWC; quotes/receipts for value; ownership proof;
willingness; adopt.

By witness type. Owner: ownership, prior condition, value/repair, no consent. · Eyewitness: who did it and
how, ADVOKATE. · Garda: scene, what observed, BWC, exhibits.
THINK: Prove it belonged to another and that they didn’t consent; pin the value/extent with photos and
quotes; establish intent or recklessness.

5

Statement Taking — Offence Playbooks (5-Part)

Theft — s.4 Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001
POINTS TO PROVE. Dishonest appropriation of property · without the consent of the owner · with intent to
deprive the owner of it.
Part

Focus & prompts (get them thinking)

1 Intro

Who the witness is; their relationship to the property (owner, staff, security).

2 Scene

Where; the property (describe, value); how it was secured/held; who had lawful
access.

3 Incident

What was taken and how (if seen) — the appropriation; that it was without consent
and dishonest; intent to permanently deprive; any concealment/flight.

4 Impact

Value and any loss; effect on the business/person; recovery (or not).

5 Close-out

CCTV (identify, preserve, produce), BWC; the item / receipts / value; ownership;
willingness; adopt.

By witness type. Owner/staff: ownership, value, no consent, whether recovered. · Eyewitness/security:
the act of taking, ADVOKATE, continuity of any recovered item. · Garda: recovery, arrest, CCTV
production, exhibits/continuity.
THINK: Nail ‘without consent’ and ‘intent to deprive’ — not just that the item is gone. Who owns it, its
value, and the CCTV that shows the taking.

6

Statement Taking — Offence Playbooks (5-Part)

Harassment — s.10 Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 (as substituted, 2024)
POINTS TO PROVE. Persistent acts — following, watching, pestering, besetting, or communicating WITH
or ABOUT the person · intentionally or recklessly · without lawful authority or reasonable excuse · that
seriously interfere with peace and privacy OR cause alarm, distress or harm · where a reasonable person
would realise it would.
Part

Focus & prompts (get them thinking)

1 Intro

Who the witness is; relationship to the suspect; the period the conduct covers.

2 Scene

The pattern — build a timeline of incidents (dates, times, places, method: in person,
phone, online, about them to others).

3 Incident

Each act described — what was done/said, how often, how it persisted; that it was
unwanted and without lawful excuse; that a reasonable person would see the effect.
Preserve messages/screenshots as exhibits.

4 Impact

The cumulative effect — serious interference with peace and privacy, or alarm,
distress or harm; fear, sleep, changes to routine, avoidance. This is central.

5 Close-out

Message logs/screenshots, call records, CCTV, BWC; any prior
warnings/complaints; willingness; adopt.

By witness type. Victim: the pattern, the impact, the persistence, why unwanted. · Independent witness:
acts they observed / were told about (note hearsay). · Garda: complaints history, exhibits, digital evidence
continuity.
NOTE. s.10 was substituted (2 Sept 2024) by the CJ(Misc Provisions) Act 2023, which also created a
distinct STALKING offence — verify the current wording and choose the right charge.
THINK: Harassment is about the PATTERN and the IMPACT, not one act. Capture persistence and the
serious interference / distress — and consider the separate offence of STALKING (s.10(2):
intentionally/recklessly causing fear of violence, or serious alarm or distress substantially affecting day-today activities).
End — Working reference, not legal advice, verify current wording before relying.

7
